warning: Origin facts come from named primary sources. pitch.dogView and whyHere are editorial judgments, not historical facts or recommendation evidence.

stories.creditLine: Designed by Paul D. Hunt at Adobe Originals, with later language work shaped through consultation and community use.

people.name: Andrew Keith Strauss
people.role: Na-Dené language consultation named by Adobe

stories.origin: Adobe's first open-source type family. The third major version expanded Greek and Cyrillic coverage and continued a long process of revising the family through real language needs.
stories.pitchDogView: It does not demand applause. Its humanity lives in open shapes, useful italics, and the feeling that ordinary information has been considered rather than styled.
stories.whyHere: A low-complexity deck system and a quiet body/utility partner for more expressive faces.
stories.limits: Neutrality can become anonymity. Platform naming, static files, and every claimed language still require tests.

primarySources.label: Adobe: What's new in Source Sans 3

stories.creditLine: Designed by Frank Grießhammer at Adobe Originals, with named collaborators across optical sizes and Greek italics.
stories.origin: A contemporary interpretation of Pierre-Simon Fournier's transitional types, conceived as a companion to Source Sans. Optical sizes let the same family become sturdier in captions and finer in display work.
stories.pitchDogView: Bookish without putting on a powdered wig. Its optical sizes make the useful point that small type and large type are different physical jobs.
stories.whyHere: Editorial titles and measured prose in film, treatment, and leave-behind work, paired with Source Sans for practical text.
stories.limits: Optical-size behaviour varies by application; the inspected mirror file is not the latest canonical release.

primarySources.label: Adobe: Source Serif gets optical sizes

stories.creditLine: Creative direction by Mike Abbink; type direction and design with Bold Monday; extended by an international team of script specialists and engineers.
stories.origin: IBM created Plex to replace borrowed corporate neutrality with a family shaped around its own history: a tension between human and machine, rational and emotional. The wider project includes many scripts made by named specialists.
stories.pitchDogView: It has the confidence of a technical drawing with just enough warmth left in the pencil line.
stories.whyHere: A one-family route for data, strategy, evidence, and collaborative decks.
stories.limits: IBM and Google Fonts packages diverge. Separate script families are separate files and cannot be claimed as one automatic multilingual system.

primarySources.label: IBM Plex design team and specifications

stories.creditLine: Designed, spaced, and kerned by Mathieu Triay, with named QA, language advice, collaborators, and Google funding.

people.name: Jérémy Landes
people.role: designer of Mayenne Sans, the open-source starting point

stories.origin: Triay describes the face through migration, hybrid identity, French and British grotesque histories, and rebuilding from existing materials. He dedicated it to his grandfather, Edgard Triay.
stories.pitchDogView: The letters feel inherited, modified, and still slightly restless. That is more interesting than a generic label like bold or quirky.
stories.whyHere: Short display work that needs cultural texture and movement, held in check by a quiet body face.
stories.limits: No italic in the inspected family; display discipline and Latin-only public scope until review.

primarySources.label: Mathieu Triay: Bricolage Grotesque story and specimen

stories.creditLine: A Braille Institute project made with Applied Design Works, Elliott Scott, Megan Eiswerth, and Letters From Sweden.

people.name: Elliott Scott
people.role: design contributor named in project metadata

people.name: Megan Eiswerth
people.role: design contributor named in project metadata

stories.origin: The original family was built around distinguishing commonly confused letterforms for readers with low vision. The 2025 Next release expanded the weights, italics, and language reach.
stories.pitchDogView: Its open counters and deliberately different shapes make clarity visible without making the page feel clinical.
stories.whyHere: A candidate for projection, phone viewing, names, and practical one-family hierarchy.
stories.limits: A typeface cannot make a deck accessible by itself, and one legibility strategy does not serve every reader. Low-vision users must review it.

primarySources.label: Braille Institute: font family and design principles

stories.creditLine: Designed at Production Type by Hugues Gentile; commissioned by Google Fonts.
stories.origin: An open-source serif made for continuous on-screen reading in content-rich environments, rather than a print face merely placed on a screen.
stories.pitchDogView: It brings a patient reading rhythm. That matters in a synopsis or treatment where the paragraph, not the title, has to hold attention.
stories.whyHere: Longer prose and leave-behind reading, paired with Source Sans for charts, labels, and navigation.
stories.limits: It still needs deck-width, optical-size, phone, data, and application tests. A reading brief does not automatically prove presentation performance.

stories.creditLine: Part of the Plex superfamily directed by Mike Abbink and Bold Monday, with a large international design and engineering team.
stories.origin: Plex Serif extends IBM's human-and-machine idea into a more literary voice while remaining part of the same designed family as Plex Sans.
stories.pitchDogView: It changes temperature without changing citizenship. That makes the serif/sans relationship feel deliberate rather than decorated.
stories.whyHere: A lower-risk editorial pairing for research, strategy, and evidence-led decks.
stories.limits: The Google Fonts file inspected here trails IBM's canonical package. Pairing and application tests must use reconciled files.

primarySources.label: IBM Plex design team and specifications

stories.creditLine: Designed by Undercase Type, Phaedra Charles, and Flavia Zimbardi.
stories.origin: A variable soft-serif display family that revisits an underappreciated strand of early twentieth-century type. Its optical size, softness, and wonk axes make that history adjustable rather than embalmed.
stories.pitchDogView: Warm, peculiar, and capable of looking both assured and slightly unruly. It gives a title a body instead of merely giving it weight.
stories.whyHere: An expressive serif route clearly different from Bricolage, paired with a quiet sans for everything practical.
stories.limits: Display only in this collection. Proportional numerals and strong formal character make it unsuitable for ordinary tables or dense body copy without contrary evidence.

primarySources.label: Undercase Type: Fraunces story and specimen
